original_abstract:
Modern steganography for digital media primarily relies on embedding payloads while minimizing a carefully defined distortion function. With the development of practical codes that operate near the theoretical payload-distortion bound, the central challenge for the steganographer has shifted toward designing distortion functions that offer high resistance to empirical statistical detection. 

In this paper, we introduce Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD), a universal distortion design applicable across multiple embedding domains. The distortion is calculated as the sum of relative changes in coefficients within a directional filter bank decomposition of the cover image. By utilizing directionality, UNIWARD concentrates embedding changes in regions that are difficult to model across multiple directions—such as textures and noisy areas—while intentionally avoiding smooth regions and clean edges. 

We evaluate the performance of UNIWARD-based methods in the spatial, JPEG, and side-informed JPEG domains using both state-of-the-art rich models and targeted attacks. Our experimental results demonstrate that UNIWARD consistently matches or exceeds the security of current state-of-the-art steganographic methods across all tested domains.
humanized_abstract:
Modern steganography in digital media is concerned with hiding information by keeping the distortion of the data as small as possible to a given distortion function. Given that steganalytic techniques are currently capable of exploiting a large subset of the possible distortion functions and that practical steganographic codes have now approached the optimal payload versus distortion performance guarantee, the steganographer's main task now focuses on defining the distortion function that is difficult to distinguish from randomness by the most common empirical statistical detectors.

In this paper, we present a new universal distortion design, called Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD), that can be effectively and efficiently applied to a wide range of embedding domains. Our distortion metric is the sum of relative coefficients variations in the coefficients of a directional filter bank decomposition of the cover image. The directionality of our design allows us to concentrate the distortions in the hardest to model areas, such as textures and random noise, while avoiding smooth surfaces and clean edges.

We evaluate the UNIWARD based steganographic schemes in spatial, JPEG and side-informed JPEG domains by using either the state of the art rich models or specialized attacks. The test results confirm that the UNIWARD based steganography schemes have on par with or even outperform the existing state of the art steganographic techniques in all three domains.

In this paper, we present a new universal distortion design, called Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD), that can be effectively and efficiently applied to a wide range of embedding domains. The test results confirm that the UNIWARD based steganography schemes have on par with or even outperform the existing state of the art steganographic techniques in all three domains.
document.input:
